name: Tucson Artifacts
description: The Tucson Artifacts refer to a collection of approximately 30 lead, zinc, and copper objects purportedly discovered in a cave near Tucson, Arizona, in the 1920s. These artifacts, primarily crosses, swords, and plaques, are inscribed with images and texts interpreted as a record of a Roman or Roman-Jewish expedition to North America during the late 8th century. Proponents argue that the artifacts demonstrate pre-Columbian contact between Europe and the Americas, specifically a lost Roman colony. The story typically involves a group of Roman soldiers and refugees who fled the collapse of the Roman Empire or were exiled, eventually making their way to the Americas. The artifacts' inscriptions are said to detail their journey, struggles, and interactions with indigenous populations. Mainstream archaeologists and historians generally dismiss the Tucson Artifacts as an elaborate hoax. The artifacts lack archaeological context, having been reportedly found by individuals with no formal archaeological training. The inscriptions are inconsistent with known Roman epigraphy and paleography. Furthermore, the metallurgical composition of the artifacts is unusual for ancient Roman metalwork. The absence of corroborating evidence, such as Roman settlements or artifacts discovered in controlled excavations, further undermines the claim of authenticity. The episode is often cited as a cautionary tale about the dangers of pseudoscientific claims and the importance of rigorous archaeological methodology.
subtitle: Alleged Roman artifacts found in Arizona.

claims.claim: Romans reached North America in the 8th century.
claims.evidence: Inscribed lead artifacts depicting Roman imagery and text.
claims.criticism: Artifacts lack archaeological context, inscriptions are inconsistent with Roman epigraphy, and metal composition is anomalous.

claims.claim: The artifacts record a lost Roman colony in Arizona.
claims.evidence: Inscriptions detailing a journey and settlement in the Americas.
claims.criticism: No corroborating archaeological evidence of Roman settlements or artifacts in the area.

claims.claim: The artifacts are genuine historical records.
claims.evidence: The complexity of the inscriptions and the apparent age of the artifacts.
claims.criticism: Expert analysis suggests the inscriptions are poorly executed and the artifacts were likely recently manufactured.

keyFigures.name: Charles E. Manier
keyFigures.role: Promoter and seller of the artifacts.

keyFigures.name: Thomas Bentz
keyFigures.role: One of the individuals claiming to have discovered the artifacts.

keyFigures.name: J. Reid Crowell
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist who investigated and debunked the artifacts.

mythologyConnections: Atlantis (as a similar lost civilization narrative)
Theories of ancient mariners and explorers

timeline.event: First reports of the discovery of the Tucson Artifacts.
Charles Manier promotes and sells the artifacts.
Archaeological investigations and initial skepticism arise.
The Tucson Artifacts remain a subject of debate and a case study in pseudoarchaeology.
